Arguments.prompt: Download US state boundary shapefiles from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip and US county boundary shapefiles from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_county_5m.zip. Unzip both archives. Load the state shapefile (cb_2018_us_state_20m.shp) using geopandas and filter to the row where the NAME field equals 'Florida'. Load the county shapefile (cb_2018_us_county_5m.shp) using geopandas and filter to the row where NAME equals 'Miami-Dade' and the STATEFP code matches Florida's STATEFP (which is '12'). Reproject both GeoDataFrames to EPSG:4326 if they are not already. Compute the bounding box of Miami-Dade County by taking its total_bounds (minx, miny, maxx, maxy). Expand this bounding box by 0.5 degrees in each direction: expanded_minx = minx - 0.5, expanded_miny = miny - 0.5, expanded_maxx = maxx + 0.5, expanded_maxy = maxy + 0.5. Create a GeoDataFrame called OuterBoundary containing a single polygon representing this expanded bounding box using shapely.geometry.box(expanded_minx, expanded_miny, expanded_maxx, expanded_maxy) with CRS EPSG:4326. Save the Florida state geometry as State.geojson, the Miami-Dade County geometry as County.geojson, and the expanded bounding box polygon as OuterBoundary.geojson. Upload all three files to the S3 data store under the folder MiamiDadeHeatmap using faasr_put_file: State.geojson to MiamiDadeHeatmap/State.geojson, County.geojson to MiamiDadeHeatmap/County.geojson, and OuterBoundary.geojson to MiamiDadeHeatmap/OuterBoundary.geojson.

Download OuterBoundary.geojson from the S3 data store folder MiamiDadeHeatmap using faasr_get_file. Load it with geopandas to obtain the expanded bounding box polygon. Extract the bounding coordinates: expanded_minx, expanded_miny, expanded_maxx, expanded_maxy from the geometry's bounds. Download the NOAA GHCND station inventory file from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/pub/data/ghcn/daily/ghcnd-inventory.txt using requests or urllib. This file is a fixed-width text file with columns: STATION (characters 1-11), LATITUDE (13-20), LONGITUDE (22-30), ELEMENT (32-35), FIRSTYEAR (37-40), LASTYEAR (42-45). Parse it accordingly. Filter rows to keep only those where ELEMENT is 'TMAX' or 'TMIN'. For each station, it must have BOTH a TMAX record and a TMIN record where LASTYEAR is greater than or equal to the current calendar year (use datetime.date.today().year). Further filter to stations whose LATITUDE falls between expanded_miny and expanded_maxy and whose LONGITUDE falls between expanded_minx and expanded_maxx. Create a GeoDataFrame with one row per qualifying station containing columns: STATION (station ID string), LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, and a Point geometry constructed from (LONGITUDE, LATITUDE) with CRS EPSG:4326. Save this as Stations.geojson and upload it to the S3 data store as MiamiDadeHeatmap/Stations.geojson using faasr_put_file.

Download Stations.geojson from the S3 data store folder MiamiDadeHeatmap using faasr_get_file. Load it with geopandas to get the list of station IDs and their geometries. Determine the target week as follows: take today's date using datetime.date.today(), subtract 28 days to get a reference date, then find the most recent Monday on or before that reference date by subtracting (reference_date.weekday()) days (where Monday=0). That Monday is week_start. The week_end is week_start plus 6 days (the following Sunday). For each station in Stations.geojson, download its full daily CSV record from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/global-historical-climatology-network-daily/access/<STATION_ID>.csv where <STATION_ID> is the station's STATION field value. The CSV has a header row with columns including DATE, TMAX, TMIN among others. Parse the DATE column as dates. Filter rows to only those where DATE is between week_start and week_end inclusive. Drop any rows where TMIN or TMAX is missing (NaN or empty). If after filtering a station has no valid rows, skip that station entirely. For stations with valid data, compute the mean of TMIN across all remaining rows and the mean of TMAX across all remaining rows. Divide both means by 10.0 to convert from tenths of a degree Celsius to degrees Celsius. Collect results into a GeoDataFrame with columns: STATION (string), geometry (Point from Stations.geojson), TMIN (float, degrees Celsius), TMAX (float, degrees Celsius), with CRS EPSG:4326. Save this as TemperatureData.geojson and upload it to the S3 data store as MiamiDadeHeatmap/TemperatureData.geojson using faasr_put_file. Also save a plain text file WeekDates.txt containing exactly two lines: the first line is week_start in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD) and the second line is week_end in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD). Upload WeekDates.txt to MiamiDadeHeatmap/WeekDates.txt using faasr_put_file.

Download the following files from the S3 data store folder MiamiDadeHeatmap using faasr_get_file: OuterBoundary.geojson, TemperatureData.geojson, State.geojson, County.geojson, and WeekDates.txt. Load OuterBoundary.geojson with geopandas to get the expanded bounding box. Extract expanded_minx, expanded_miny, expanded_maxx, expanded_maxy from its geometry bounds. Load TemperatureData.geojson with geopandas; it has columns STATION, geometry (Point), TMIN (float, degrees Celsius), TMAX (float, degrees Celsius). Load State.geojson and County.geojson with geopandas for boundary overlays. Read WeekDates.txt: the first line is week_start (YYYY-MM-DD) and the second line is week_end (YYYY-MM-DD). Parse both as datetime.date objects. Build a 100x100 interpolation grid: grid_lon = numpy.linspace(expanded_minx, expanded_maxx, 100), grid_lat = numpy.linspace(expanded_miny, expanded_maxy, 100), then grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d = numpy.meshgrid(grid_lon, grid_lat). Extract station coordinates as points array: numpy.column_stack([station_longitudes, station_latitudes]) where station_longitudes = [geom.x for geom in TemperatureData.geometry] and station_latitudes = [geom.y for geom in TemperatureData.geometry]. Use scipy.interpolate.griddata with method='cubic' to interpolate TMIN values onto the grid: grid_tmin = scipy.interpolate.griddata(points, tmin_values, (grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d), method='cubic'). Similarly interpolate TMAX: grid_tmax = scipy.interpolate.griddata(points, tmax_values, (grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d), method='cubic'). Create a matplotlib figure with two side-by-side subplots using fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(18, 8)). For the left subplot (ax1): use ax1.contourf(grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d, grid_tmin, levels=20, cmap='Blues', alpha=0.8). Add a colorbar labeled 'Temperature (C)'. Overlay station scatter points using ax1.scatter(station_longitudes, station_latitudes, c=tmin_values, cmap='Blues', edgecolors='black', zorder=5). Plot Florida state boundary lines from State.geojson on ax1 using state_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax1, color='black', linewidth=1). Plot Miami-Dade County boundary lines from County.geojson on ax1 using county_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax1, color='red', linewidth=1.5). Set ax1 x-limits to (expanded_minx, expanded_maxx) and y-limits to (expanded_miny, expanded_maxy). Set ax1 x-ticks and y-ticks at intervals of 0.5 degrees using numpy.arange. Label ax1 x-axis as 'Longitude' and y-axis as 'Latitude'. Title ax1 as 'Minimum Temperature Heatmap (C)'. For the right subplot (ax2): use ax2.contourf(grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d, grid_tmax, levels=20, cmap='Reds', alpha=0.8). Add a colorbar labeled 'Temperature (C)'. Overlay station scatter points using ax2.scatter(station_longitudes, station_latitudes, c=tmax_values, cmap='Reds', edgecolors='black', zorder=5). Plot Florida state boundary lines on ax2 using state_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax2, color='black', linewidth=1). Plot Miami-Dade County boundary lines on ax2 using county_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax2, color='red', linewidth=1.5). Set ax2 x-limits to (expanded_minx, expanded_maxx) and y-limits to (expanded_miny, expanded_maxy). Set ax2 x-ticks and y-ticks at intervals of 0.5 degrees. Label ax2 x-axis as 'Longitude' and y-axis as 'Latitude'. Title ax2 as 'Maximum Temperature Heatmap (C)'. Format week_start as a human-readable string like 'Monday, January 6, 2025' and week_end as 'Sunday, January 12, 2025'. Set the figure supertitle using fig.suptitle('Temperature Heatmap for Miami-Dade County for week of <week_start_formatted> to <week_end_formatted>', fontsize=14). Call plt.tight_layout(). Save the figure as TemperatureHeatmap.png using plt.savefig('TemperatureHeatmap.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight'). Upload TemperatureHeatmap.png to the S3 data store as MiamiDadeHeatmap/TemperatureHeatmap.png using faasr_put_file. Also re-upload all intermediate files to confirm they are present: upload OuterBoundary.geojson to MiamiDadeHeatmap/OuterBoundary.geojson, State.geojson to MiamiDadeHeatmap/State.geojson, County.geojson to MiamiDadeHeatmap/County.geojson, Stations.geojson to MiamiDadeHeatmap/Stations.geojson, TemperatureData.geojson to MiamiDadeHeatmap/TemperatureData.geojson, and WeekDates.txt to MiamiDadeHeatmap/WeekDates.txt using faasr_put_file for each.
